title: Latin Cognates - AL Endings
description: Words ending in -AL convert directly to Spanish with stress on final syllable. Introduction to 'es' (is/it is).

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: English and Spanish are two related languages. English is considered a Germanic language, but roughly half of its vocabulary comes from Latin. We can convert this Latin vocabulary over to Spanish really easily, because Spanish is practically modern Latin.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So we can identify Latin words in English and, with a few simple rules, convert them to Spanish. This gives us around 3,000 words.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: One of these rules is that words ending in A-L tend to work directly in Spanish. You just stress the final syllable. So for example, the English word normal — in Spanish, that's normal.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say metal?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Metal. Good. Spanish has five vowels, and each one always makes the same sound: A, E, I, O, U. Keep them short and steady.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: The word for 'is' in Spanish is es. E-S. And this also means 'it is', 'she is', 'he is', or even 'you are' when talking to somebody formally.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So now we can say 'he is normal' or 'she is normal'. How would you say that?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es normal. Good. One tip: in English, the S in 'is' is pronounced like a Z. In Spanish, always keep it as a clean S sound.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say legal?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Legal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it is legal'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es legal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say illegal?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ilegal. Good. In Spanish the I is like 'ink' and the E is like 'elephant'. Ilegal.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So how would you say 'it is illegal'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es ilegal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: What if you want to say 'he is liberal'? You do not need the word for 'he' if you know who you are talking about. You can just use es. How would you say it?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es liberal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Now Spanish is much more phonetic than English. The letters represent the same sound. So with 'natural', you don't just want to change the accent and say it like English. You want to think how it's written and pronounce the letters as they are.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you think you would say natural in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Natural. Good. Remember to pronounce every letter as written - A sounds like ah, and U sounds like oo. Natural. And stress the final syllable.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: If you want to say 'it isn't' or 'it's not', the word for 'not' or 'don't' is the same as the word for 'no'. And the word for 'no' in Spanish is the same as English, but you pronounce it like Spanish: no.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: You put 'no' before 'es' to say 'it is not'. How would you say 'it is not'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No es. Good. So now we can say things like 'it's not normal'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it's not normal'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No es normal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say ideal? Remember: I as in 'ink', E as in 'elephant', A as in 'apple'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ideal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it's ideal'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es ideal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it's not ideal'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No es ideal. Perfect.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say fatal?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Fatal. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say colonial?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Colonial. Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say cultural? Think about how it's written.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Cultural. Good. Notice how each vowel gets its own clean sound. Cultural.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say annual? And cut out that 'y' sound that comes in English. Focus on how it's written.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Anual. Very nice.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say dental?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Dental. Good. Tooth in Spanish is diente. So this is coming from dental. If you look up tooth in the dictionary and find diente, rather than treating it as random vocabulary to memorize, you can relate it back to dental.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say verbal?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Verbal. Good. So knowing 'verbal', you can probably guess the word for 'verb' in Spanish.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you think you say verb in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Verbo. Good. With vocabulary, it's okay to guess based on patterns. If you're a little off, a native speaker will usually still understand you, and then you can adjust. That's one of the fastest ways to build vocabulary.

outro.text: You've now got a pattern that works for hundreds of English words. Any word ending in A-L - you already know how to say it in Spanish.
Try one that we haven't covered. Think of any English word ending in A-L - maybe 'emotional', or 'professional', or 'digital' - and say it in Spanish with the stress on the end.
You can now describe things in a conversation. Something is normal, something is ideal, something is not legal. That's real language you can use today.
Next time, we'll take this same idea and look at words with different endings. You'll see how quickly your vocabulary grows once you know what to look for.
